content:
Here are some great guidelines when you are starting a free-write warm-up exercise:

Keep your fingers moving on the keyboard for 5 minutes.
Write everything that comes to mind, even seemingly unrelated comments like, "I hate writing. This is too hard. I don't think I would like eating squid if I had the chance."
Don't self-edit. Get it all down without worrying about how it looks or whether or not it is correct.
Be outrageous. Use vocabulary and descriptions that sound overboard, silly, or absurd. Make comparisons and connections to other subjects (even if they seem at first glance to be irrelevant or unrelated).
Keep writing, no matter what, until your first 5 minutes is up, and then stop.
Instructions
Perform the following tasks:

On a Word document or the text entry box:

Complete this week's prompt:
This week, you are learning about credible sources and the audience they are speaking to. Regardless of what media you watch or read, it is all biased. Refer to the chart belowLinks to an external site. to determine if your favorite media group is left-leaning, more centric or right-leaning. Now, read a current event headline to your favorite news outlet (left- or right-leaning). You may want to read it once or twice. Free write your impressions of the article. Then, go to a news outlet that is opposite your favorite news outlet and do the same. For example, if you choose to read a current event in USA Today, after freewriting about it, go to Fox News and read about the same event and free-write about it from that perspective. What have you learned from two perspectives when reading about the same event? What does this tell you about credibility and audience?
